coredata.dc:title: Complementary effect of combined bacterial-chemical pretreatment to promote enzymatic digestibility of lignocellulose biomass
coredata.dc:creator: Si, Mengying
Liu, Dan
Liu, Mingren
Yan, Xu
Gao, Congjie
Chai, Liyuan
Shi, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Chemical pretreatment partially modified the structure of lignocellulose to enhance saccharification, leaving unaltered factors to limit further hydrolysis. To overcome these limitations, a biostrategy involving co-pretreatment combining bacteria with a chemical process was developed. A significant complementary effect was observed in specific co-pretreatments, e.g., ligninolytic bacteria enhanced acid pretreatment and saccharolytic bacteria enhanced alkaline pretreatment. Specifically, the ligninolytic bacterium Pandoraea sp. B-6 selectively removed the acidolysis-caused residual lignin and enhanced sugar release by 40.9% to 772.0 mg g−1 compared with that of acid-treated rice straw. After most of the lignin was removed, sugar release from alkali-treated RS was further improved by 31.8% to 820.2 mg g−1 via the saccharolytic bacterium Acinetobacter sp. B-2 through decrystallization. In the complementary mechanism, the active sites produced by chemical cleavage facilitated the bioprocess and further enhanced saccharification. This complementary mechanism provides a novel foundation for designing a rational combination pretreatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Complementary effect
Combination pretreatment
Functional bacteria
Delignification
Decrystallization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of the various pretreatment on the chemical composition of the RS; Cel: cellulose, Hcel: hemicellulose, Lig: lignin.
Effect of the various pretreatment on the enzymatic hydrolysis of the RS.
Characterization of RS by XRD and XPS.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: SLC = (O/Csample − O/Ccellulose)/(O/Clignin − O/Ccellulose); the O/Csample term: O/C ratio of the analyzed sample, O/Ccellulose: 0.83, O/Clignin: 0.33.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para:
C–C = AC–C /(AC–C + AC–O + AC
O/O–C–O + AC–C
O ); AC–C : the peak area of the C–C in C 1s spectrum, AC–O : the peak area of the C–O in the C 1s spectrum, AC
O/O–C–O : the peak area of the CO/O–C–O in the C 1s spectrum, AC–C
O : the peak area of the C–CO in the C 1s spectrum.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para:
C–O = AC–O /(AC–C + AC–O + AC
O/O–C–O + AC–C
O ); AC–C : the peak area of the C–C in the C 1s spectrum, AC–O : the peak area of the C–O in the C 1s spectrum, AC
O/O–C–O : the peak area of the CO/O–C–O in the C 1s spectrum, AC–C
O : the peak area of the C–CO in the C 1s spectrum.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para:
CO = AC
O /(AC–O–C + AC
O ); AC–O–C : the peak area of the C–O–C in the O 1s spectrum, AC
O : the peak area of the CO in the O 1s spectrum.

head.ce:title: Complementary effect of combined bacterial-chemical pretreatment to promote enzymatic digestibility of lignocellulose biomass

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgy and Environment, Central South University, Changsha 410083, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chinese National Engineering Research Centre for Control & Treatment of Heavy Metal Pollution, Changsha 410083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chinese National Engineering Research Centre for Control & Treatment of Heavy Metal Pollution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Membrane Separation and Water Science & Technology, Ocean College, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Membrane Separation and Water Science & Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Environmental Science and Engineering, School of Metallurgy and Environment, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, PR China. Tel.: +86 0731 88830875; fax: +86 0731 88710171.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical pretreatment partially modified the structure of lignocellulose to enhance saccharification, leaving unaltered factors to limit further hydrolysis. To overcome these limitations, a biostrategy involving co-pretreatment combining bacteria with a chemical process was developed. A significant complementary effect was observed in specific co-pretreatments, e.g., ligninolytic bacteria enhanced acid pretreatment and saccharolytic bacteria enhanced alkaline pretreatment. Specifically, the ligninolytic bacterium Pandoraea sp. B-6 selectively removed the acidolysis-caused residual lignin and enhanced sugar release by 40.9% to 772.0 mg g−1 compared with that of acid-treated rice straw. After most of the lignin was removed, sugar release from alkali-treated RS was further improved by 31.8% to 820.2 mg g−1 via the saccharolytic bacterium Acinetobacter sp. B-2 through decrystallization. In the complementary mechanism, the active sites produced by chemical cleavage facilitated the bioprocess and further enhanced saccharification. This complementary mechanism provides a novel foundation for designing a rational combination pretreatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Increased energy demands have made lignocellulosic biomass a highly attractive renewable source of chemicals, materials, and fuels (Xu et al., 2014). The abundance of lignocellulosic reserves may enable a transition to a bio-based economy from the petrol-based industry (Serrano-Ruiz et al., 2011). Thus, biorefinery processes based on sugar-platform involving enzymatic saccharification and subsequent fermentation are emerging as most prominent technologies (Rastogi and Shrivastava, 2017). However, this proposed transition from carbohydrates to bioethanol has not been fully commercialized because of the recalcitrant structure of lignocellulose and low conversion rates (Jin et al., 2012; Si et al., 2018a). Hence, promotion of this bioconversion is of ongoing interest to researchers.

Heterogeneous lignocellulose is composed of carbohydrate (cellulose, hemicellulose) and lignin network matrix, matures into cellular complexes, and provides natural integrity to protect itself from microbial attack and enzymatic deconstruction (Si et al., 2018a; Singh et al., 2015). The factors contributing to the extreme resistance of lignocellulose for enzymatic hydrolysis mainly include hydrophobic lignin content, hemicellulose content (Pihlajaniemi et al., 2016), accessible surface area of the substrate (Meng et al., 2017), degree of cellulose polymerization (Singh et al., 2015), and cellulose crystallinity (Peciulyte et al., 2015). Therefore, pretreatment of lignocellulose to disrupt substrate recalcitrance is critical for bioconversion (Yan et al., 2017). Numerous chemical strategies have been designed to selectively overcome some of these factors to enhance the enzymatic digestibility of biomass. However, other factors limit further saccharification and restrict chemical pretreatments.

Bacterial species exhibit rapid growth and are easy to genetically manipulate, which is considered as a promising and environment-friendly strategy (Bugg et al., 2011). Studies have examined the use of bacteria in biostrategies to further promote chemical pretreatment (Dai et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2018; Zhuo et al., 2018). Biostrategies involving combination pretreatment have mainly employed ligninolytic strains and focused on further delignification to generate purer carbohydrates for saccharification. However, individual chemical pretreatment affects different factors and produces substrates with different limitations depending on the specific process and mechanism. Thus, the lignin fraction is not the limiting factor in all chemical pretreatments, suggesting that ligninolytic strains are not the optimal choice for developing biostrategies to overcome the limitation of all chemical pretreatments. Therefore, saccharolytic bacteria show great potential for use in biostrategies. However, few methods involving co-pretreatment have been developed. Additionally, there is no theoretical guidance for bacterial-chemical pretreatment in the design of a rationally combined strategy.

Alkali and acid pretreatments involve different mechanisms to enhance the saccharification of the biomass, particularly with different effects on lignin fraction (Jin et al., 2016; Wildschut et al., 2013). In this study, alkali and acid treatments combined with saccharolytic and ligninolytic strains, respectively, were performed to pretreat rice straw (RS). The enhancement of enzymatic digestibility was evaluated. The effects of co-pretreatment were comprehensively analyzed. Our study provides insight into co-pretreatment of lignocellulose and guides the design of a rational biostrategy for overcoming the limitations of chemical pretreatment and further facilitating bioconversion.

Air-dried RS (Shandong, China) was ground into powder and sifted using a 60-mesh griddle. The obtained RS samples were stored at room temperature and used as the pretreatment feedstock. Four bacterial strains, Acinetobacter sp. B-2, Bacillus sp. B-3, Pandoraea sp. B-6, and Comamonas sp. B-9 isolated from steeping fluid of erosive bamboo slips were used in this study. In our previous studies, the optimum conditions for carbon resource degradation were comprehensively analyzed. For all bacterial growth with corresponding compounds as the sole carbon source, the optimum pH was 7, optimum temperature was 30 °C, and optimum shaker speed was 150 rpm. Herein, bacteria cultivation was conducted under the optimized conditions. Seed cultures were grown overnight in Luria-Bertani (LB) broth medium at 30 °C until the logarithmic growth phase was reached at an optical density at 600 nm (OD600) of 1.0. The obtained bacterial culture was used for biological treatment.

Different types of carbon resources (including glucose, xylose, galactose, arabinose, mannose, ferulic acid, sinapic acid, and p-coumaric acid) at a concentration of 1 g L−1 were respectively added to individual flasks containing 100 mL of mineral salt medium (MSM). Next, 5 mL of the bacterial culture was centrifuged to harvest the bacterial cells and then inoculated into the MSM with a carbon source. After 2 days of incubation at 30 °C at a speed of 150 rpm, the bacterial culture was collected. To evaluate the utilization of carbon sources by bacteria, the OD600 of the bacterial culture was investigated. Cultivation was conducted in a rotary shaker at 30 °C at a speed of 150 rpm. The composition of the MSM (g L−1 de-ionized water) was as follows: 2 g L−1 (NH4)2SO4, 1 g L−1 K2HPO4, 0.2 g L−1 MgSO4, 0.01 g L−1 CaCl2, 0.015 g L−1 FeSO4, 0.01 g L−1 MnSO4, 1 g L−1 KH2PO4, at a pH of 7.0.

Seven grams of RS were mixed with 70 mL of ethanol–water solution (65:35, v/v) containing HCl (0.5 wt%, indicated as H), FeCl3 (0.5 wt%, indicated as F), and NaOH (0.5 wt%, indicated as N). The mixture was transferred into a microwave digestion tank (KDB-III) and statically treated at 400 W of microwave irradiation in a microwave digestion system (P70F20L-DG, Galanz, Guangdong, China) for 10 min. The treated RS residues were collected and thoroughly washed with de-ionized water, and then dried at 50 °C for further treatment. The chemical treated RS was named as chemical reagent-RS, e.g., H-RS was the HCl-based treated RS.

Biopretreatment was carried out in a 250-mL Erlenmeyer flask containing 1 g of treated RS, 100 mL of MSM, and 10 mL of seed culture. After 2 days of incubation at 30 °C at a speed of 150 rpm, the sample was collected as described above. The co-treated RS was named as chemical reagent + name of bacterium-RS, e.g., H2-RS was the RS pretreated by HCl, following by B-2 pretreatment.

Typical enzymatic hydrolysis was carried out as follows: 0.5 g of RS sample, 20 mL of 50 mM citric acid buffer (pH 4.8), cellulase (12 FPU g−1), antibiotic (40 μg mL−1), and cycloheximide (30 μg mL−1) were mixed and incubated at 50 °C in a rotary shaker at a speed of 110 rpm for 48 h. Samples were collected and centrifuged for sugar analysis (Cheng et al., 2017). Commercial cellulase (Cellic CTec2, Novozymes, Bagsværd, Denmark) was used for enzymatic hydrolysis.

Different treated RS samples were collected for compositional and physicochemical analyses. The chemical compositions were measured as described by Teramoto et al. (2008). To better understand the mechanisms of bioconversion, the crystallinity of RS samples was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (TTRIII, Rigaku Co., Tokyo, Japan) (Chai et al., 2014; Yan et al., 2014) and calculated using the following equation:
Crystallinity index
CrI
=
I 002
-
I
amp
/
I 002
CrI / Cel = CrI /
cellulose content
where I002 is the intensity of the (0 0 2) peak at 2θ = 22.5° and Iamp is the background intensity at 2θ = 18.3°. The cellulose content was the cellulose content in the RS.

Additionally, the surface elemental composition and surface lignin coverage (SLC) of RS samples were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermo 2500XI, Waltham, MA, USA) (Chandra et al., 2016; Qi et al., 2018). SLC was calculated from the O/C ratios according to the following equation:
SLC =
O /
C sample
- O
/
C cellulose
/
O /
C lignin
- O
/
C cellulose
where the O/Csample term is the O/C ratio of the analyzed sample and O/Ccellulose and O/Clignin are the theoretical O/C ratios of pure cellulose (0.83) and lignin (0.33) (Nan et al., 2018).

Recalcitrant lignocellulose chiefly contains carbohydrates and lignin, suggesting that the bacterial strain employed for lignocellulose pretreatment should degrade cellulose, hemicellulose, or lignin with high efficiency. Four bacterial strains, including Acinetobacter sp. B-2 (B-2), Bacillus sp. B-3 (B-3), Pandoraea sp. B-6 (B-6), and Comamonas sp. B-9 (B-9), were used, and bacterial growth was investigated by measuring the OD600. Monomeric precursors (including glucose, xylose, galactose, arabinose, mannose, ferulic acid, sinapic acid, and p-coumaric acid) of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin were selected as the sole carbon sources in the present study. The potential of the strains for use in biopretreatment was preliminarily evaluated. The typical monosaccharide of cellulose (glucose) and hemicellulose (xylose, galactose, arabinose, mannose) were well utilized by B-2, showing a greater than 1.80-fold increase in the OD600 after 2 days of incubation, suggesting that B-2 possesses can degrade carbohydrates. However, B-2 hardly used any of the typical lignin monomer for growth (less than 0.05-fold increase of OD600). Therefore, B-2 was identified as a saccharolytic bacteria species. In contrast, the OD600 of B-6 increased by 1.45-fold in ferulic acid and p-coumaric acid medium, but showed minimal growth with the typical monosaccharides of cellulose and hemicellulose (less than 0.15-fold increase in OD600) as the sole carbon source. Therefore, B-6 exhibited outstanding potential to selectively remove the lignin fraction in lignocellulose and was identified as a ligninolytic bacteria. Both monosaccharides and monolignol compounds were effectively used by B-3 for growth, showing a more than 0.82-fold increase in OD600. Additionally, B-9, which showed minimal use of both monosaccharides and lignin monomers with a negligible OD600 increase, was used as a control.

To investigate the effect of the strains during the individual biopretreatment process, changes in the chemical composition in RS were measured (Fig. 1 ). The untreated RS contained 46.4% cellulose, 35.8% hemicellulose, 18.7% lignin, and 3–4% other matter (e.g., ash) as a control. After H-based acid pretreatment, the hemicellulose content obviously reduced to 24.4%, accompanied by a slight decrease in lignin content (from 18.7% to 14.0%). As reported previously, amorphous pentosan in the lignocellulosic matrix was more susceptible to acidolysis (Song et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2017). Additionally, the partial lignin was removed by acidolysis, retaining the residual lignin fraction in RS. Specifically, the lignin content significantly decreased to 8.4% in N-RS, which was attributed to lignin solvation via nucleophilic cleavage of lignin phenolic alkyl-aryl ethers by alkaline pretreatment (Narron et al., 2016). The hemicellulose content slightly decreased to 30.0%, suggesting that the highly ordered crystalline structure of cellulose was only minimally degraded. Cellulose was highly preserved in the chemical-treated RS (53.7% and 53.1% in H-RS and N-RS, respectively). In addition, F-RS showed no obvious change in chemical composition (48.4% cellulose, 33.8% hemicellulose, and 14.0% lignin) because of the weak acidity of FeCl3.

After biopretreatment, changes in the components of biotreated RS varied depending on the effects of the bacteria. As expected, the chemical composition of all biotreated RS changed obviously except for that treated with B-9, which is consistent with the results of monomeric compound utilization. In detail, the lignin fraction was decreased from 8.4–14.0% to 7.6–10.4% by B-6 pretreatment, thereby generating purer carbohydrates (83.9–85.9%). It also well confirmed that selective delignification was achieved by B-6 treatment. The lignin content slightly decreased from 8.4% to 7.6% in N6-RS. This was because the remaining lignin had a recalcitrant structure in N-RS and resisted removal by B-6. Following saccharolytic bacterial (B-2) pretreatment, the hemicellulose content was clearly decreased to 19.5–28.2% from 24.4 to 33.8%, while the lignin content was nearly unchanged. This indicates that amorphous hemicellulose was easily utilized by saccharolytic bacteria. A similar phenomenon was observed following B-3 pretreatment, suggesting that B-3 preferentially removed or degraded hemicellulose during biotreatment. The weight losses following biopretreatment were also measured. The results showed that less than 6.5% of the RS weight was lost during treatment with saccharolytic bacteria (B-2 or B-3). According to composition analysis, the weight losses of the carbohydrate were less than 4.9%. This negligible loss in carbohydrate did not directly reduce the actual amount of biofuel. Therefore, mineralization of saccharolytic bacteria had limited effects on consuming the essential carbohydrate in the natural biomass to reduce the sugar yield.

To evaluate the efficiencies of various co-pretreatments, the RS residues were subsequently hydrolyzed with cellulase CTec2 (loading of 12 FPU g−1) for 48 h. Untreated samples only yielded 91.6 mg g−1 sugars (Fig. 2 ). As expected, a low increase in enzymatic digestibility was always accompanied by a negligible change in the chemical composition, e.g., in F-treated and B-9 treated RS. In contrast, H-based acid pretreated significantly increased saccharification of the treated RS to yield 547.9 mg g−1 sugars. The sugars exhibited an improved yield of 622.4 mg g−1 in N-based alkaline pretreated RS. The digestibility of RS was further enhanced by biopretreatment except for by B-9. The biotreatment based on H-treatment promoted the saccharification by 21.5–40.9% to 665.8–772.0 mg g−1 as compared with the H-RS. For bacteria-enhanced N-treatment, sugar release from co-treated RS increased by 12.0–31.8% to 697.3–820.2 mg g−1 compared with that of N-RS. Notably, maximum enhancement (increased by 40.9%) was achieved by combining B-6 pretreatment with H-based acid pretreatment. However, B-6 pretreatment slightly increased the saccharification of N-RS by 12.0%. Interestingly, the opposite results were observed for saccharolytic bacterium B-2-enhanced chemical pretreatment. An higher increase (31.8%) by N-based co-treatment and lower increase (21.5%) was achieved by H-based co-treatment.

Generally, alkali conditions not only destroyed the hydrophobic restriction of lignin for cellulases accessing cellulose (Ding et al., 2012; Meng et al., 2015) but also eliminated nonproductive adsorption sites on lignin-carbohydrate complexes (Chen et al., 2013; Leu and Zhu, 2013; Pihlajaniemi et al., 2015; Yang and Wyman, 2006) by removing lignin and altering the complex lignin–hemicellulose network (Bali et al., 2015). Thus, the unchanged cellulose with a highly crystalline structure limited saccharification. Biotreatment by the saccharolytic bacterium B-2 showed the potential to further enhance digestibility, achieving a significant increase in sugar yield. In the H-treated process, acidolysis mainly deconstructed the amorphous carbohydrate and slightly removed the lignin (Rastogi and Shrivastava, 2017). The residual lignin still inhibited hydrolysis by binding to cellulase and blocking enzyme progression along the glucose chains (Zhang and Lynd, 2004). Residual lignin served as the main factor limiting determination of the hydrolysis of H-RS. At this point, employing the ligninolytic bacterium B-6 effectively increased the digestibility of H-RS. Thus, a significant “complementary effect” was observed when chemical and biological pretreatment were combined. Specifically, saccharolytic bacteria further improved the digestibility of the N-RS by selectively depolymerizing polysaccharide to compensate for the shortage obtained by N-treatment. Ligninolytic bacteria effectively removed the lignin to eliminate the inhibition and enhance the enzymatic hydrolysis of H-RS.

Crystallinity showed recalcitrance of the cellulose content in RS (Chai et al., 2018). To further verify the point of the “complementary effect” during co-pretreatment, the changes in cellulose crystallinity of the RS after various pretreatments were determined and expressed as crystallinity index/cellulose (CrI/Cel). The main peaks of 16.0° and 22.5° in the X-ray diffraction patterns were ascribed to the diffraction plane of cellulose I (1 0 1) and (0 0 2), respectively. Their positions did not vary following treatment.

The untreated RS with a CrI/Cel content of 1.06 contained two natural cellulose structures, a crystalline form and amorphous form. After H-treatment, the CrI/Cel content increased to 1.07, presumably because of dissolution of amorphous cellulose by acidolysis. The CrI/Cel content slightly decreased to 1.03 by B-3, indicating that the cellulose content in H-RS was resistant to B-3 treatment. Additionally, the CrI/Cel content of H2-RS was significantly decreased to 0.86, indicating that the crystal structure of cellulose was partly degraded by B-2. However, the corresponding lower increase in sugar yield (21.5%) obtained by H2-RS showed that the reduction in the crystallinity of cellulose did not directly enhance digestibility. The change in CrI/Cel content of H6-RS (1.04) was not clear compared to that of H-RS, whereas an obvious increase (40.9%) in sugar yield was observed. These results further confirmed that the crystalline structure was not the key factor affecting the RS after H-treatment. Thus, sequential biopretreatment employed to enhance acid treatment did not change the crystal structure of cellulose.

For N-based treatment, the negligible decrease in CrI/Cel content (1.04) of N-RS confirmed that cellulose in the crystalline form and amorphous form was not susceptible to degradation under mild alkali conditions, which is consistent with the results of component analyses. The CrI/Cel content slightly reduced to 1.02, presumably because of the resistance of cellulose to bacterial erosion by B-3. Notably, the crystalline structure (0.92) of N2-RS was much lower than that of N-RS, which may be attributed to the decrystallization of B-2. Accordingly, this improvement in digestibility significantly increased the sugar yield by 31.8% to achieve the maximum carbohydrate conversion (820.2 mg g−1) in N2-RS. The unchanged crystallinity did not enhance digestibility. The negligible change (increase by only 1.92% as compared to N-RS) observed in the crystallinity of N6-RS ultimately resulted in only 12.0% enhancement in sugar yield. Thus, the crystalline structure was an important limitation to N-treatment. Therefore, mineralization of saccharolytic bacteria exhibited a limited effect on consuming the essential carbohydrate, but significantly modified the crystalline structure to enhance saccharification and resulted in a significant “complementary effect”.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra can be used to characterize the SLC as the O/C ratio (Nan et al., 2018; Qi et al., 2018). According to survey scans, RS mainly contained carbon and oxygen with C 1s and O 1s peaks at 286.6 and 533.6 eV, respectively. In detail, four peaks at 284.8, 286.4, 287.9, and 289.0 eV were observed in the C 1s spectrum, which were attributed to C–C, C–O, CO/O–C–O, and C–CO bonds, respectively (Si et al., 2018b; Zhang et al., 2012). The O 1s spectrum showed two peaks, including C–O–C bonds at 533.0 eV and CO at 531.8 eV (Yang et al., 2014). Lignin is a complex phenolic macromolecule with a higher content of C–C type carbon atoms and lower content of C–O type carbon atoms compared with the polysaccharide structure (Nan et al., 2018). Additionally, the CO peak in the O 1s spectrum was considered as unrelated to pure polysaccharide (including cellulose and hemicelluloses), which had a linear structure of glucose or pentose linked by β-1,4 bonds (Ju et al., 2013). As listed in Table 1 , untreated RS exhibited 89.4% SLC with 79.2% C–C type and 16.4% C–O type carbon atoms. For CO in the O 1s spectrum, 31.7% was mainly derived from the lignin fraction. After N-treatment, the C–C and CO content directly decreased to 52.4% and 11.9%, respectively, resulting in a significant decrease in SLC from 89.4% to 41.4%. In contrast, a milder decrease was observed in H-RS (60.4% C–C, 22.2% CO, and 61.8% SLC). These results confirmed that the SLC was effectively removed by N-treatment, while lignin migrated to the biomass surface during H-treatment. The SLC with C–C, C–O, and CO content only minimally changed after biotreatment with B-2 and B-3. This further confirmed that the significant enhancement in digestibility by saccharolytic bacteria enhanced alkali-treated RS did not benefit from lignin removal. As observed for treatment by B-6, the SLC dramatically decreased to 39.1% and 23.0% in H6-RS and N6-RS, respectively, retaining only 52.3% and 34.2% C–C type carbon atoms, respectively. The CO content decreased to 18.9% in H6-RS and 4.6% in N6-RS. Following enzymatic hydrolysis, lignin removal notably enhanced the digestibility of H6-RS rather than N6-RS. This confirmed that the remaining lignin served was the limiting factor after H-treatment and not N-treatment. Thus, the sequential biopretreatment of ligninolytic bacteria effectively removed the lignin to further enhance the enzymatic digestibility, resulting in a “complementary effect”.

Based on all the results above, the biomechanism of complementary effect was rationally speculated. Because of the limited effects of biotreatment (Dai et al., 2015), lignocellulolytic microorganisms were employed as a subsequent biostrategy to enhance the chemical pretreatment rather than solo biopretreatment. However, synergistic effects were not obtained for all combined bacterial-chemical pretreatments. In this study, the data for component analysis, enzymatic hydrolysis, crystalline, and surface lignin distribution analysis all suggested a significant complementary effect caused by specifically combined chemical-bacterial pretreatment to further promote enzymatic digestibility, e.g., ligninolytic bacteria enhanced acid pretreatment and saccharolytic bacteria enhanced alkaline pretreatment. Generally, chemical pretreatment selectively attacked the inter- and intra-molecular linkages among lignocellulose to break down its structural compactness and heterogeneity, and synchronously exposed abundant ends of the molecular chains. Thus, chemical pretreatment was effective and saccharification was improved as the severity of pretreatment conditions increased. However, limited efficiency in further enhancement was observed when excessively increasing the pretreatment conditions. An environment-friendly biostrategy is needed. To achieve further enhancement through the biostrategy, the exposed ends of the molecular chains appear to be important. Generally, multiple reactions were catalyzed by enzymes or metabolic pathways in the bioprocesses of the functional microorganisms. Some bacteria employed for biopretreatment may produce intermediates with the similar ends to those exposed by chemical cleavage. These compounds were regarded as inhibitors and formed feedback inhibition for the enzymes to result in inefficient biopretreatment (Zhai et al., 2018). This explains why a lower increase was observed in N6-RS and H2-RS. In contrast, when the other functional bacteria were employed, the ends of the chains might serve as active sites to bind the binding domain of the functional enzymes, and then effectively promote the bioprocess, e.g., ligninolytic bacteria enhanced acid pretreatment and saccharolytic bacteria enhanced alkaline pretreatment. The biopretreatment was notably facilitated by the exposed ends produced by chemical cleavage, which further improved saccharification of the chemical treated biomass. Thus, this significant complementary effect of combination treatment promoted the enzymatic digestibility of lignocellulose.

A significant complementary effect was observed following combined bacterial-chemical pretreatment. The active sites of chemical cleavage facilitated the bioprocess to enhance saccharification of the chemically treated biomass. Specifically, enzymatic hydrolysis of H-RS was dramatically increased by 40.9% after delignification via ligninolytic bacteria. Saccharolytic bacteria enhanced saccharification of the N-RS by 31.8% through decrystallization. The complementary effect effectively overcame the limiting factors of the chemical process to further improve the saccharification of lignocellulose. This complementary mechanism provides a novel perspective for designing a logical combined bacterial-chemical pretreatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Science and Technology Project of Hunan Province (2017RS3011), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M612594).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2018.10.036.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The effect of alkaline pretreatment methods on cellulose structure and accessibility
